story:
I knew a sailor named Bruce, old Bruce, a flagrant malcontent who stirred up trouble on shore and hunted for meaning on the black water. Night after night he sailed out under a moon that looked like a cold coin, and morning after morning he came back hollow-eyed, empty-handed, a golden sun shining on his ill contempt like a lamp over a bog. He was searching for a God, an answer, a reason to keep breathing.

One stormy night he stumbled into my bar soaked from eyebrow to boot, eyes wide like a goat at the butcher’s gate. “A STORM, Brutish!” he croaked through yellow teeth. He said he’d found it at last—some forgotten solution—and vowed to sleep a hundred nights, dreaming only of flat, blue calm, until the world woke for war again. Before I could ask what he meant, he crumpled and broke his bulbous nose on the floor. The bar was empty. I let him sleep.

Morning came in slanting like a dream. When he woke, he said, “Brutish, it hit me like a goat’s head in the backside. I’ve been flagrant all my life, pretending I know what I’m doing because I’m scared. I think everyone else has the secret—but nobody knows what they’re doing. Still, the ship must be sailed, the waters explored, and the traveling never still. Storms are born from men like me, chasing gold or God. I haven’t found either, but it’s something to hold. So I go on digging like a fool till I meet the architect—or I lie down in the dark and sleep a hundred nights.” He left, then came back the next morning, drunk as a fish, chattering nonsense, and ordered hot ham. I fried it.

On a crisp morning he staggered in, fish-eyed, panting like the air was too thick for lungs. “Water,” he begged. I handed him a glass; he doused his steaming hat and asked for another. That one he drank like a camel meeting a well. He dropped into a chair like a rusted anchor, sleepless honey eyes burning like a lighthouse looking for a ship, a damsel, or a ghost. “It’s an oven in here, Brutish! More water!” He gulped a third, then stood, fists wiping at something no one else could see. “Keep your distance! I’ve got a hammer and I’ll use it, you diseased bag of ham!” He mimed plucking it from his boot, and for a moment he seemed to shine as if he held a golden hammer, a bronze statue under October light. He squared himself like a bullfighter. “That’s it, Devil!” Then his shoulders sagged. “I have to go, Brutish. I can’t breathe.” He hurried to the door, sunlight bursting onto stone outside. From the street he shouted to every window, “I am not a man! I am a fish! I belong to the sea!” His voice echoed like his wet footprints. It was the last time I heard him.

I should have told him I admired him—his wildness, his fool’s courage. But I didn’t. Story says he died that night, far out on slick water, trying to light a pipe while waves slapped his face of stone. I prefer to think he’s still out there, morning sun warming his burned lips, smiling, well fed, with a clean glass of water in hand.
